narrative: As an Asian male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including a solid LSAT score of 36 and an undergraduate GPA of 2.90. However, the university's admission model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 94.6% chance that you would fail it—far too high a risk for them to approve your application, leading to the rejection. This prediction hinged most strongly on your academic decile rankings from earlier years in school, particularly your Year 3 performance, which placed you in the bottom decile compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing around the 5th or 6th decile. That low Year 3 ranking was the single biggest factor pushing the model toward forecasting bar failure, as it signaled potential challenges in sustaining strong grades over time. Your Year 1 decile, also at the bottom, reinforced this concern as the second-most influential element, suggesting early academic hurdles that the model sees as persistent risks. On a more positive note, your LSAT score provided the strongest counterbalance, nudging the prediction toward success because it's close to the average of 36.7 and demonstrates solid test-taking ability under pressure. Compared to typical students in the dataset—who often have higher deciles around 5.5 to 5.7—your early rankings pulled you into a high-risk category despite those strengths. The model weighs these patterns from past students to avoid admitting those statistically prone to bar struggles, even for determined applicants like you. While factors like your full-time status aligned with the vast majority of students (over 92%), they didn't outweigh the academic red flags. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, as even modest improvements there could dramatically shift future predictions. You might also target LSAT retakes for a slight edge or seek targeted bar prep resources tailored to students with similar profiles. Many in your position have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps—your background shows resilience, so persistence will pay off. Reapplying after strengthening those early academic markers could open doors at this or other schools.
